name: Supply Chain Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The six courses form a tight, coherent supply-chain set: analytical foundations (EM 602 Management Science, IE 604 Advanced Engineering Statistics), logistics (EM 640 Distribution Logistics), the domain capstone (IE 659 Supply Chain Engineering), plus cost economics (IE 618) and project delivery (EM 636). The pieces reinforce each other around SCM design and optimization.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an applied capstone/project or inventory-and-operations course so the analytical and logistics threads culminate in an integrative design experience.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain=0 means every course is entered independently and missingCourseCount=0 confirms no dangling references; the flat graph keeps the certificate accessible, but EM 602 and IE 604 assume undergraduate calculus and statistics (stated as external prerequisites), so quantitative readiness is implied rather than scaffolded within the credential.
dimensions.suggestion: Recommend a sequence (e.g., IE 604/EM 602 before IE 659) and state the undergraduate quantitative prerequisites explicitly so students self-assess readiness.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes are present in the data, so target competencies (network design, logistics optimization, SC cost analysis) are not stated for prospective students.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 4-5 certificate outcomes tied to the courses (e.g., design a distribution network, model supply-chain costs, apply statistical methods to quality/operations).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits=30 is present but sits oddly against a six-course (18-credit) list, suggesting the total is inherited from a degree rather than the certificate itself; for a focused certificate the six 3-credit courses are well-chosen, but the credit total is not transparent about how many courses are actually required.
dimensions.suggestion: Correct/clarify the required credits for the certificate (e.g., 4 courses / 12 credits, or confirm the 30-credit path) so the stated total matches the course list.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Descriptions are present and informative (e.g., IE 659 covers supply-chain design, implementation, and transportation/distribution networks), but there are no requirement groups, no core/elective labeling, and the credit total conflicts with the list, so structure is not fully legible.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a certificate overview and group courses into required core versus electives, reconciling the credit total with the number of required courses.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The set reflects solid contemporary SCM practice: distribution logistics (EM 640), supply-chain network design (IE 659), management science/optimization (EM 602), and quality statistics (IE 604). It is somewhat classical, however, missing digital supply chain, analytics/AI, and resilience/sustainability content that now define the field.
dimensions.suggestion: Add or cross-list a supply-chain analytics/digital-SCM course (demand forecasting, optimization software, digital twins) to modernize the credential.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are specific and content-rich (e.g., EM 602 lists linear programming, transportation/assignment problems, Markov chains, and queueing; IE 659 covers supply-chain design and distribution networks), not thin placeholders.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are clear where present, EM 602 states 'undergraduate calculus and probability and statistics' and IE 604 references 'IE 331 or equivalent'; missingCourseCount=0 confirms no dangling references, though these point outside the graduate catalog.

missingTopics.rationale: EM 602 and IE 604 teach the math, but no course centers on modern SC analytics tooling (forecasting, network-optimization solvers, dashboards) that employers expect.
The set covers distribution (EM 640) and SC design (IE 659) but lacks a dedicated inventory-control/operations course, a core SCM pillar.
Post-pandemic sourcing disruptions have made resilience and risk mitigation essential; no listed course addresses it.
Strategic sourcing and supplier relationship management are core SCM functions absent from the current course list.

emergingTrends.trend: Digital supply chain and digital twins
emergingTrends.rationale: Real-time visibility and simulation of supply networks are transforming practice; IE 659's design content could be extended with digital-twin modeling.

emergingTrends.trend: AI/ML for demand forecasting and logistics
emergingTrends.rationale: Machine-learning demand planning and route optimization are now standard; EM 640/EM 602 quantitative content is a natural host for these methods.
Carbon-aware logistics and circular-economy sourcing are rising priorities that IE 618 cost economics and EM 640 logistics could incorporate.
Geopolitical risk is driving nearshoring/reshoring decisions that benefit from the optimization tools EM 602 already teaches.

topSuggestions: Reconcile the credit total (statedTotalCredits=30 vs. a 6-course list) and label required core versus electives.
Add a supply-chain analytics / digital-SCM course to modernize the classical optimization-and-logistics core.
Add an inventory/operations and a resilience-and-risk course to close core SCM gaps.
Publish certificate learning outcomes tied to the courses (network design, cost modeling, statistical methods).
Recommend a course sequence and state undergraduate quantitative prerequisites so applicants can gauge readiness.
